book.title: Never Split the Difference: Negotiating As If Your Life Depended On It
book.author: Chris Voss (with Tahl Raz)
book.domain_fit_note: Highest-value book for actual message copy. Voss's method is short, emotional, turn-based verbal exchange where you can't see the other person — exactly P2P marketplace chat. Mirroring, labeling, calibrated How/What questions, and the Ackerman concession ladder map almost 1:1 onto the Offer/Best-Offer button, lowballers, 'is this still available?', ghosting, and bundle haggling. Crisis/hostage framing is irrelevant; the conversational mechanics are directly usable.

core_thesis: Negotiation is emotional and System-1 driven, irrational in predictable patterns, not rational problem-solving. The winning move is tactical empathy — recognizing and vocalizing the other side's emotions to make them feel understood, safe, and in control, which lowers defenses and lets you steer them. Use active-listening tools (mirroring, labeling, silence), seek 'No' and 'That's right' instead of chasing 'Yes', ask calibrated How/What questions that make the counterpart solve your problem, and anchor + concede on a disciplined ladder (Ackerman). Mantra: 'No deal is better than a bad deal' and 'never split the difference.'

plays.what_it_means: Repeat the critical last one-to-three words of what the buyer said, with an upward 'help me understand' inflection, then go silent.
plays.why_it_matters: Triggers the bonding/isopraxism instinct ('we're alike'), makes the buyer re-word and elaborate, buys time, and surfaces their real strategy without disagreeing.
plays.when_to_use: Vague or probing buyer messages; when you want them to reveal budget or motive before committing to a number.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When the buyer has made a clean firm offer you want to accept; overuse in one thread reads robotic.
plays.marketplace_mapping: First reply in DMs before any number is named; reacting to a lowball comment under a listing; in eBay/Posh offer messages to get the buyer to justify their number.
plays.example_buyer_message: Would you take a lot less for this? It's been listed a while.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 2 'Mirroring' — 'repeat the last three words (or the critical one to three words).'

plays.what_it_means: Name the buyer's likely emotion or hesitation aloud as a neutral statement, never starting with 'I', then stay silent.
plays.why_it_matters: Labeling a feeling moves brain activity from the amygdala to rational areas, defusing negatives and reinforcing positives; a shortcut to feeling understood.
plays.when_to_use: Buyer is hesitant, frustrated, suspicious, or stalling; clearing emotional barriers before talking price.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: Don't use 'I'm hearing that' ('I' raises guard); don't stack a question right after — let it sit.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Responding to a hesitant 'hmm not sure' DM; defusing a buyer annoyed about shipping cost or slow reply.
plays.example_buyer_message: Idk, $40 feels like a lot for something secondhand.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: It sounds like you want to be sure you're getting real value before you commit.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 3 'Labeling' — 'It seems like / It sounds like / It looks like'; Lieberman amygdala study.

plays.what_it_means: List the worst things the buyer could think/say about your offer, then say them first to take the sting out.
plays.why_it_matters: Denying a negative gives it credence; voicing it first disarms it and triggers the buyer to insist the opposite is true.
plays.when_to_use: When you must deliver something the buyer won't love — firm price, no further discount, declining a bundle, a flaw disclosure.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When there's no real objection to pre-empt (manufacturing fake negatives reads weird).
plays.marketplace_mapping: Opening a 'sorry, firm price' message; declining a lowball; disclosing a small defect before they find it in photos.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: You're probably going to think I'm being stubborn or that I don't want to make a deal — I get it. The truth is I've already priced this below what similar ones sell for, so I can't go to $15. But I'd love to find a number that works.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 3 'Do an Accusation Audit' — Anna's case; 'list every terrible thing your counterpart could say about you.'

plays.principle: Calibrated 'How am I supposed to do that?'
plays.what_it_means: Respond to a lowball or demand with a deferential open question that forces the buyer to confront your constraints and solve your problem.
plays.why_it_matters: Creates 'forced empathy' and the illusion of control; the buyer often talks themselves up or withdraws the demand without you saying 'No.'
plays.when_to_use: Direct lowball offers; demands for free shipping / extra discount; 'knock off $X.'
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When said with an aggressive tone (becomes an accusation); against analyst buyers who want data — pair with a fact.
plays.marketplace_mapping: The #1 default reply to a lowball Offer on eBay/Poshmark/Depop; counter to 'free shipping or no deal.'
plays.recommended_agent_reply: I appreciate the offer — but how am I supposed to do $20 when I paid more than that for it myself? What were you hoping to spend?
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 7 'Calibrate Your Questions' & Ch. 5 — 'How am I supposed to do that?'; 'forced empathy.'

plays.what_it_means: Ask questions designed to get a 'No' — it makes people feel safe and in control and opens the conversation.
plays.why_it_matters: 'Yes' early is a defensive counterfeit; 'No' reaffirms autonomy, lowers guard, and gets them engaged.
plays.when_to_use: Cold or guarded buyers; re-opening a stalled thread; instead of pushy 'can you buy today?' asks.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When you need a binding commitment now — pair with a follow-up that converts.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Replacing 'Do you want to buy it?' with 'Is this no longer something you're interested in?'; reviving a buyer who saved the item.
plays.example_buyer_message: (buyer asked a question then went quiet)
plays.recommended_agent_reply: Have you given up on this one?
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 4 ''No' Starts the Negotiation'; 'Is now a bad time?' vs 'Do you have a few minutes?'

plays.what_it_means: To revive a ghosting buyer, send one short 'No'-oriented question that implies you'll move on.
plays.why_it_matters: Plays on loss aversion + autonomy; the buyer replies to correct you and reclaim control rather than be abandoned.
plays.when_to_use: Buyer who made an offer or asked about availability then went silent 24h+.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: More than once per buyer (becomes nagging); on a buyer who already clearly declined.
plays.marketplace_mapping: The anti-ghosting nudge; also for a buyer sitting on a sent counteroffer about to expire.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: Have you given up on this jacket? Totally fine if it's no longer right — just want to know whether to keep holding it for you.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 4 'Email Magic: How Never to Be Ignored Again' — 'Have you given up on this project?'

plays.principle: Drive to 'That's right' with a summary
plays.what_it_means: Paraphrase the buyer's situation + label their underlying emotion so the only response is 'That's right' (NOT 'you're right').
plays.why_it_matters: 'That's right' is genuine buy-in; 'you're right' means they want you to stop talking and nothing will change.
plays.when_to_use: Stuck negotiations, repeat hagglers, or before asking for the close — to confirm true alignment.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: As a manipulation to fake agreement on something you haven't actually understood.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Before sending a final counter; converting a chatty browser who keeps asking but not buying.
plays.example_buyer_message: I love it but I'm trying not to overspend this month and I've been burned by sizing online before.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: So it sounds like you really want the piece, you just need to feel confident the fit is right and that you're not blowing your budget on a guess.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 5 'Trigger a 'That's Right' With a Summary' (Sabaya); ''That's Right' vs 'You're Right'' (Brandon).

plays.principle: Anchor emotions before the number (loss aversion)
plays.what_it_means: Before naming a price/concession, set low expectations and make inaction feel like a loss.
plays.why_it_matters: People take bigger risks to avoid a loss than to get a gain; 'you might lose this' beats 'you could gain this.'
plays.when_to_use: When introducing a firm price, a small discount you won't exceed, or a genuine 'last one' situation.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: Fake scarcity (guardrail) — only claim a genuine loss (real last item, real other interest).
plays.marketplace_mapping: 'A couple others have it saved' (only if true); framing your counter as the deal that's about to disappear; end-of-listing relist warning.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: Totally — no rush from me. Just a heads up, two other people have this saved and I'm relisting Sunday, so I didn't want you to miss it if it's the one you wanted.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 6 'Anchor Their Emotions' (UAE $500/day contractors); 'persuade them they have something concrete to lose.'

plays.what_it_means: Don't volunteer a discount number; get the buyer to name their price first so you see their hand.
plays.why_it_matters: Their first offer is often higher than your floor; going first risks winner's curse / underselling.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When you want a fast sale at a set number and the buyer is an informed pro.
plays.marketplace_mapping: The classic 'what's your best price?' DM — bounce it back; eBay Best-Offer where you can wait for their offer.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: I'm open to a fair offer — what did you have in mind?
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 6 'Let the Other Guy Go First... Most of the Time' (Chandler/Jerry).

plays.what_it_means: When forced to give a number, cite a range anchored to comparable sales, with the low end being what you actually want.
plays.why_it_matters: Ranges seem less aggressive yet pull the counterpart toward the higher end / their limit; named comps add authority.
plays.when_to_use: Justifying your asking price; responding when you can't dodge naming a figure.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When you have no real comps to cite (don't fabricate).
plays.marketplace_mapping: 'Similar ones on Depop/eBay sold for $X-$Y' to defend price; countering 'it's too expensive.'
plays.example_buyer_message: $45 is way too much for this.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: I hear you — though sold listings for this exact style are running $42-$58 right now, so $45 is already at the low end of that.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 6 'Establish a Range' — Jerry/'$130,000-$170,000'; Columbia bolstering-range study.

plays.what_it_means: When stuck on 'how much?', trade things cheap to you but valuable to them (or ask for things valuable to you).
plays.why_it_matters: Breaks the rigid price-only frame; expands the pie and lets both sides feel they won.
plays.when_to_use: Gap between your floor and their ceiling; bundle requests; buyers who want 'a deal.'
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When the buyer only cares about absolute price and you have no flexible terms.
plays.marketplace_mapping: 'I can't drop the price but I'll cover shipping / add a matching accessory / bundle a second item'; ask buyer to take 2 items for a small bump.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: I can't do $25 on its own — but if you grab the tee you also liked, I'll do both for $38 shipped. How does that sound?
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 6 'Pivot to Nonmonetary Terms' (Memphis Bar magazine cover).

plays.what_it_means: Make offers/counters end in non-round numbers; round numbers read as placeholders, odd ones as calculated and final.
plays.why_it_matters: A precise figure signals you've reached your real limit and is psychologically harder to negotiate off of.
plays.when_to_use: Final counteroffer; 'firm' price you won't move from.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: Tiny-dollar items where precision looks silly.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Counter at $37 not $35; 'best I can do is $42.50 shipped.'
plays.recommended_agent_reply: I can come down to $43 — that's genuinely the lowest the numbers work for me.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 6 'When You Do Talk Numbers, Use Odd Ones' & Ch. 9 ('$4,751').

plays.what_it_means: Set your target floor, then concede on a shrinking ladder so each drop is smaller, ending on an odd number + a tiny non-monetary add-on. (Seller-side inversion of the 65/85/95/100 buyer model.)
plays.why_it_matters: Decreasing increments make the buyer feel they squeezed your last drop; reciprocity makes them counter; you land on your real number.
plays.when_to_use: Multi-message haggles with a committed buyer; high-value items worth the back-and-forth.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: Low-value quick flips; buyers who want one firm price.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Listed $50, floor $42. Counter $48, then $44, then $43, then $42.50 + 'free tracked shipping.' Each concession smaller than the last.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: (round 1) I can do $48. (round 2) Okay, $44. (round 3) $43 is really pushing it, but $42.50 and I'll cover shipping — that's my limit.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 9 'Ackerman Bargaining' — 65% anchor, raises to 85/95/100%, decreasing increments, nonround final + nonmonetary item.

plays.principle: Don't compromise / no deal beats a bad deal
plays.what_it_means: Refuse the reflexive 'split the difference'; be ready to walk (relist) rather than take a loss.
plays.why_it_matters: Compromise is driven by fear, not goals ('one black and one brown shoe'); the ready-to-walk mindset removes neediness, which is leverage.
plays.when_to_use: Buyer pushes 'just meet me halfway'; thin-margin items; serial discounters.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When the item is aging, you need cash/space, and 'halfway' still clears your floor — know your floor first.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Coach the seller: relisting / cross-listing is your BATNA; declining a bad offer is fine. Counter 'split it?' with terms or a small odd concession, not the midpoint.
plays.example_buyer_message: You're at $40, I'm at $30, let's just do $35.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: I get the instinct to split it, but $35 doesn't quite work for me. I can do $38 today, otherwise no worries — I'm happy to hold out.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 6 'Don't Compromise' (mismatched shoes) & Ch. 9 'No Neediness: the ready-to-walk mindset.'

plays.principle: Use your own name ('the Chris discount')
plays.what_it_means: Introduce yourself by name to humanize yourself and create forced empathy before/while negotiating.
plays.why_it_matters: Makes you a real person rather than a faceless seller, so the buyer is less comfortable lowballing or ghosting you.
plays.when_to_use: Opening a DM thread; warming up a haggler; any time the interaction feels cold/transactional.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When it would feel forced in a one-line offer exchange.
plays.marketplace_mapping: 'Hey, I'm Sam — thanks for checking out the listing!' as the opener; signing messages with your name.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: Hey! I'm Sam, thanks for the interest. What were you thinking budget-wise and I'll see what I can do?
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 9 'Use your own name... the Chris discount' & 'forced empathy.'

plays.what_it_means: Get the buyer to confirm the agreement three times in different ways before closing, to surface flakiness/lies.
plays.why_it_matters: It's hard to fake conviction three times; uncovers a counterfeit 'yes' before you ship or hold an item.
plays.when_to_use: Before marking sold, holding an item, or accepting an offer from a wishy-washy buyer.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: With a fast, clearly committed buyer (over-confirming kills momentum).
plays.marketplace_mapping: Confirm offer (1), summarize to a 'that's right' (2), ask a 'how' implementation question about payment/shipping (3) — flushes out no-shows.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: Great — so we're set at $42 shipped? [1] Sounds like you want it in time for the weekend? [2] How would you like to handle checkout so I can get it out today? [3]
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 8 'The Rule of Three' — agree to the same thing three times.

plays.principle: Calibrated questions to handle the verbal attack (no counter-punch)
plays.what_it_means: When a buyer is rude/aggressive, don't counterattack — disarm with a calibrated 'What/How' question and an 'I'-statement boundary.
plays.why_it_matters: Lowers the buyer's 'hostage mentality'; keeps you rational; refocuses on the issue not the person.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: Harassment/threats/scam attempts — escalate to safety/block flow instead of negotiating.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Reply to 'this is overpriced garbage, $10 take it or leave it'; aggressive shipping-dispute messages.
plays.example_buyer_message: Are you serious with this price? Total ripoff.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: Sounds like the price feels off to you — what would feel fair from your side? (I'm sorry if it came across high; happy to talk it through.)
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 7 'How Not to Get Paid' & 'bite your tongue'; Ch. 9 ''I' Messages.'

plays.principle: Positive/playful voice (default) + late-night DJ voice (for the firm line)
plays.what_it_means: Default to a warm, smiling, easygoing tone; switch to a slow, calm, downward-inflected 'DJ' tone only for the one line that's non-negotiable.
plays.why_it_matters: Positive mood makes buyers collaborate not fight; the calm declarative tone conveys 'not up for discussion' without triggering pushback.
plays.when_to_use: Whole thread = playful; the firm-price / final-offer line = DJ voice.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: Don't default to the assertive/direct voice — it triggers defensiveness.
plays.marketplace_mapping: [INFERRED] Copilot sets message warmth high by default and flattens/shortens the one sentence stating the firm price.
plays.example_buyer_message: Come on, just knock off another $10.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: Haha I wish I could! But $43 is my firm number on this one.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: Ch. 2 'The Voice' — three tones; positive/playful default, late-night FM DJ voice for points not up for discussion.

communication_tactics.name: Mirror
communication_tactics.purpose: Get the buyer to elaborate and reveal strategy/budget.
communication_tactics.overuse_risk: Sounds robotic or mocking if repeated every turn.
communication_tactics.bad_example: Mirroring every single sentence in a row.

communication_tactics.name: Label
communication_tactics.purpose: Name and defuse the buyer's emotion.
communication_tactics.overuse_risk: Stacking labels with no pause; using 'I' which raises guard.
communication_tactics.good_example: It seems like you've been burned by sizing before.

communication_tactics.name: Accusation audit
communication_tactics.purpose: Pre-empt the buyer's objections to take their sting out.
communication_tactics.overuse_risk: Inventing fake negatives; over-apologizing into weakness.
communication_tactics.good_example: You'll probably think I'm being firm here...
communication_tactics.bad_example: Listing every flaw until you talk yourself out of the sale.

communication_tactics.name: Calibrated How/What question
communication_tactics.purpose: Make the buyer solve your problem; remove aggression.
phrase_patterns: What about this works for you?
What did you have in mind?
communication_tactics.overuse_risk: Aggressive tone turns it into an accusation.
communication_tactics.good_example: How am I supposed to do $20?

communication_tactics.name: 'No'-oriented question
communication_tactics.purpose: Create safety/autonomy and re-engage a silent buyer.
communication_tactics.overuse_risk: Using the ghost-nudge repeatedly becomes nagging.
communication_tactics.good_example: Have you given up on this jacket?
communication_tactics.bad_example: Do you want to buy it or not?

communication_tactics.name: Summary to 'That's right'
communication_tactics.purpose: Achieve true buy-in before the close.
phrase_patterns: So it sounds like ___, and ___, is that right?
communication_tactics.overuse_risk: Faking a summary you don't believe.

communication_tactics.name: Emotional anchor / loss frame
communication_tactics.purpose: Inflate loss aversion to prompt action.
phrase_patterns: I didn't want you to miss it
before I offer it to someone else
communication_tactics.bad_example: 100 people are about to buy this! (false)

communication_tactics.name: Strategic umbrage / poised no
phrase_patterns: I'm sorry, that just doesn't work for me
I don't see how that would ever work

communication_tactics.name: Use your name
communication_tactics.good_example: I'm Sam, thanks for the interest!

objection_material.handling: Don't counter the number; deflect with 'How am I supposed to do that?' + accusation audit, let them re-anchor up.
objection_material.sample_reply: I appreciate the offer, but how am I supposed to do $20 when these sell for $45? What were you really hoping to spend?

objection_material.handling: Label the concern, defend with a comp range, pivot to terms; never split the difference.
objection_material.sample_reply: It sounds like you want to be sure it's worth it — sold ones are running $42-$58, so $45 is already low. If price is the sticker, I could bundle something to add value.

objection_material.handling: Treat as buying signal; warm + name yourself, then a calibrated question to move to commitment (avoid dead 'yes it's available').
objection_material.sample_reply: Yes it is! I'm Sam — were you looking to grab it now or did you have a question about it first?

objection_material.handling: Normative/range leverage: acknowledge, then differentiate with facts (condition, authenticity, shipping).
objection_material.sample_reply: Totally fair to compare — what's making theirs cheaper? Mine's the authenticated, better-condition one and ships next day, which is why it's priced where it is.

objection_material.handling: Accusation audit + label the fear, then proof (extra photos, receipts). Forced empathy via your name.
objection_material.sample_reply: You might be worried it's not as described — totally reasonable online. I'm Sam; happy to send extra close-ups or the receipt so you can buy with confidence.

objection_material.handling: Label the suspicion without defensiveness; provide authentication proof; humanize with your name.
objection_material.sample_reply: Sounds like you want to be sure it's genuine before committing — smart. Here's the receipt and tag close-ups; I'm Sam and I stand behind every item.

objection_material.handling: Calibrated question + pivot to terms (absorb shipping instead of cutting price).
objection_material.sample_reply: How would it work better for you — should I build shipping into the price so it's one flat number?

objection_material.handling: Pivot to non-monetary / expand the pie; use Ackerman on the bundle total.
objection_material.sample_reply: Love a bundle — grab both and I'll do $38 shipped instead of $44. That's my best on the two.

objection_material.handling: The 'No'-oriented re-engagement question that implies you'll move on (loss aversion + autonomy).
objection_material.sample_reply: Have you given up on this one? No worries either way — just want to know if I should keep holding it.

objection_material.handling: Don't counter-punch; bite your tongue, disarm with a calibrated question + poised 'I' boundary; de-escalate.
objection_material.sample_reply: Sounds like the price feels way off to you — what would feel fair? I'm happy to talk it through, but I can't do $10.

objection_material.handling: [INFERRED — not in book] Treat as safety, not negotiation; politely decline and keep it on-platform.
objection_material.sample_reply: I'd rather keep everything through the app so we're both protected — happy to finalize here!
objection_material.source_locator: INFERRED (safety overlay; book does not cover)

buyer_psychology: Buyers decide emotionally (System 1) then rationalize (System 2); framing/delivery shapes the rationalization more than facts. (Ch. 1)
'Yes' is often counterfeit — an escape hatch to end the conversation; 'Maybe' is worse. Real signals are 'No' (engagement) and 'That's right' (buy-in). (Ch. 4-5)
People crave autonomy and control; giving them the right to say 'No' calms them and makes them collaborate. (Ch. 4)
Loss aversion: a buyer acts harder to avoid losing the item than to gain it — 'you might miss this' beats 'you could have this.' (Ch. 6)
Fairness is an emotional trigger; buyers reject good-for-them deals that feel unfair and weaponize 'that's not fair' to extract concessions. (Ch. 6)
Buyers anchor on the first number and adjust insufficiently; extreme/odd numbers move their expectations. (Ch. 6)
Pronoun tells: overusing 'they/my partner/the budget' may mean an off-screen decision-maker or distancing from a lie. (Ch. 8)
Three buyer styles — Analyst (data, slow, hates surprises), Accommodator (relationship, chatty, may over-promise), Assertive (wants to be heard, time=money); treat them how they need. (Ch. 9)
seller_psychology: Chasing 'Yes' / being 'nice': sellers push for quick agreement and over-accommodate, producing counterfeit yeses and ghosting. Counter: seek 'No'/'That's right', set boundaries. (Ch. 4)
Splitting the difference reflexively out of fear, not goals. Counter: enforce a known floor; offer odd concessions or terms, not the midpoint. (Ch. 6)
Neediness for a sale loses leverage. Counter: surface the seller's BATNA (relist, cross-list, hold) so they can walk. (Ch. 9)
Going first / naming their lowest price when asked 'best price?' gives away leverage. Counter: bounce it back. (Ch. 6)
Counter-punching rude buyers tanks the deal. Counter: bite-your-tongue + calibrated question. (Ch. 7)
Talking too much / making it about themselves (the Daryl call) prevents the buyer from owning the decision. Counter: ask, summarize, go silent. (Ch. 4)
Treating deadlines as enemies (rushing to relist or accept before end-of-listing). Counter: deadlines cut both ways; patience is leverage. (Ch. 6)
Round-number anchoring invites further haggling. Counter: use odd, precise numbers. (Ch. 6)
ignore_list: Hostage/crisis/kidnapping operational specifics (proof-of-life questions, SWAT, surrender rituals) — used only as source for the underlying conversational mechanic.
Enterprise/B2B, M&A, salary-raise, and career-mentor negotiations — wrong stakes/relationship model for casual P2P resale.
In-person / body-language reading (7-38-55, Pinocchio nose, steepled fingers) — limited use in text-only chat; pronoun/word-count tells partially portable.
Negative leverage via real threats ('destroy your reputation') — too toxic/risky for marketplace chat; Voss himself warns against it.
Deep 'religion'/worldview Black-Swan excavation — too high-effort for a $30 transaction; lightweight version (similarity, named comps) retained.
Multi-party 'team behind the table' dynamics — rarely applicable to a single casual buyer.
guardrail_flags: Fake scarcity / fake urgency: the loss-aversion anchor (play 08) must use only TRUE losses; do not fabricate 'others are buying' / 'relisting tomorrow.' Flag urgency/scarcity copy for truthfulness check.
Manipulative 'Fair': weaponizing 'we just want what's fair' or guilt is flagged; prefer the positive use ('tell me if you ever feel I'm being unfair'). (Ch. 6)
Strategic umbrage / feigned anger: only real, controlled emotion works and faking backfires; do not coach performative anger toward buyers. (Ch. 9)
Negative leverage / threats: do not generate threats (reputation, reviews, reporting) as a bargaining tactic — safety/abuse risk. (Ch. 10)
Disingenuous niceness: scripted plastic warmth reads as a manipulative salesman; keep tone authentic. (Ch. 4)
Over-engineering low-value chats: gate Ackerman/Rule-of-Three by item value/effort — applying them to a $6 item annoys buyers.
